coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of oleic acid-modified Ag@ZnO core-shell nanoparticles into thin film composite membranes for enhanced antifouling and antibacterial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiujing
Chen, Yingbo
Feng, Xianshe
Hu, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Yufeng
Liu, Lei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Ag@ZnO-Oleic acid (OAc)/polyamide (PA) thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were prepared by interfacial polymerization with dispersing hydrophobic Ag@ZnO-OAc core-shell nanoparticles in trimesoyl chloride (TMC) solutions. The Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticle structure and interactions between OAc and TMC were confirmed by TEM, XPS and FTIR. Surface morphology, roughness, chemical composition, hydrophilicity and charges of the synthesized membranes were characterized by SEM, FTIR, contact angle and zeta potential analyses. The results showed that incorporating the Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles into the PA layer improved chemical and physical properties of the TFN membranes. Compared with the nanoparticle-free TFC membrane, the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membrane showed a considerably higher salt rejection, and a lower flux decline rate when tested with filtration of BSA solution as a model foulant and a higher flux recovery with water flush. In addition, the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membrane showed excellent antibacterial performance against E. coli and S. aureus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Core-shell nanoparticles
Thin film nanocomposite membrane
Anti-biofouling
Anti-bacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ag-OAc and Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles
Assessment of membrane antifouling and anti-bacterial properties
Releasing of silver ions (Ag+) from the Ag@ZnO-OAc TFN membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag and Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles, and (B) HRTEM images of Ag and Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles.

(A) FTIR spectra of Ag@ZnO nanoparticles before (a) and after (b) heat treatment and (c) Ag@ZnO nanoparticles after heat treatment in TMC-hexane solution, respectively, and (B) schematic illustration of Ag@ZnO nanoparticles after heat treatment.
XPS C 1s and O1s spectra of the raw NPs (before heat treatment) and modified NPs (after heat treatment).
The XPS full spectra of TFC and TFN membranes.
Schematic diagram for the interfacial polymerization process of TFN membranes.
XPS O 1s spectra of the TFC and TFN membranes.
Surface images of TFC and TFN membranes.
Cross-sectional images of TFC and TFN membranes.
AFM images of TFC and TFN membranes.
Surface zeta potential of TFC and TFN membranes.

(A) Water flux and salt rejection of the TFC and the TFN membranes with different amounts of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs (feed solution: 1000 mg/L Na2SO4 and NaCl), and (B) time-dependent normalized flux of TFC and TFN membranes during BSA filtration.
Antifouling parameters: (A) flux recovery ratio (FRR), (B) total flux decline ratio (DRt), (C) reversible flux decline ratio (DRr) and (D) irreversible flux decline ratio (DRir).
(A) Bacterial viability of the TFC and TFN membranes; (B) releasing rates of Ag+ ions from the N30 TFN and Ag TFN membranes.
Binding energy and area percentage of deconvoluted XPS C 1s and O 1s peaks of the raw NPs (before heat treatment) and modified NPs (after heat treatment).
Surface elemental percentage, O/N ratio and area percentages of deconvoluted O 1s peaks for the TFC and TFN membranes based on XPS spectra.
Thickness of PA layer, surface roughness and water contact angle of TFC membranes before and after modification.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Root mean square (RMS) surface roughness obtained from AFM.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Water contact angle on the membranes measured.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Apparent water contact angle calculated from the Wenzel equation (i.e., cosΘ∗ = r cosΘ, where r is roughness ratio measured by AFM).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface elemental percentage for Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs based on XPS spectra.
Comparison of the antifouling and bactericidal performance of TFN membranes.

1000 ppm BSA, 12 h, FRR 91.9%, DRt 24.7%, DRr 16.5%, DRir 8.2%
E. coli, 106 CFU mL−1, under UV irradiation, BR 98.2%
1000 ppm BSA, 3 h, FRR 94.4%, DRt 10.7%, DRr 5.6%, DRir 5.1%
E. coli, S. aureus, 105 CFU mL−1, BR 99.9%

head.ce:title: Incorporation of oleic acid-modified Ag@ZnO core-shell nanoparticles into thin film composite membranes for enhanced antifouling and antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, N2L 3G1, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Membrane Materials and Membrane Applications, Tianjin Motimo Membrane Technology Co. Ltd., Tianjin, 300042, PR China

Tianjin Motimo Membrane Technology Co. Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Center of Optical Materials and Chemistry, Changchun, 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Ag@ZnO-Oleic acid (OAc)/polyamide (PA) thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were prepared by interfacial polymerization with dispersing hydrophobic Ag@ZnO-OAc core-shell nanoparticles in trimesoyl chloride (TMC) solutions. The Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticle structure and interactions between OAc and TMC were confirmed by TEM, XPS and FTIR. Surface morphology, roughness, chemical composition, hydrophilicity and charges of the synthesized membranes were characterized by SEM, FTIR, contact angle and zeta potential analyses. The results showed that incorporating the Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles into the PA layer improved chemical and physical properties of the TFN membranes. Compared with the nanoparticle-free TFC membrane, the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membrane showed a considerably higher salt rejection, and a lower flux decline rate when tested with filtration of BSA solution as a model foulant and a higher flux recovery with water flush. In addition, the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membrane showed excellent antibacterial performance against E. coli and S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane technology is playing an increasingly important role in seawater desalination [1,2], water purification [3,4] and wastewater treatment [5,6]. Thin film composite (TFC) membranes are one of the most energy-efficient technologies for water treatment. They have the advantages of simple preparation without adding toxic substances and changing the nature of the separation material, strong adaptability, and stable operation at room temperature [7].

At present, many research efforts are still on the fabrication of TFC membranes with high permeability, antifouling capacity and antimicrobial property. The formation of an foulant layer on the membrane surface will result in a decline in permeation flux, shortened service life and increased operating cost due to increased mass transfer resistances and membrane cleaning required. Based on the types of foulants deposited on the membrane surface, membrane fouling can be divided into three categories: inorganic contamination, organic contamination and microbial contamination [8]. Inorganic contamination includes deposition of calcium carbonate and fouling of inorganic colloids. Organic contamination mainly includes humic acid and biomacromolecular foulants (e.g., proteins). Microbial contamination includes accumulation of microorganisms and formation of biofilms on the membrane surface. For water treatment with nanofiltration, membrane fouling is mainly caused by organic contamination and microbial contamination, and microbial contamination is often very serious.

Over the past few years, nanomaterials have been used to improve membrane performance by forming mixed matrix membranes and thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes. In TFN membranes, the nanomaterials are incorporated into the functional skin layer during interfacial polymerization. The use of antibacterial nanoparticles in TFC can help resolve the bio-fouling problems. There are two types of such nanomaterials: one is contact-based agents (e.g., carbon-based nanomaterials) and the other is release-based agents (e.g., metals and metal oxides) [9]. Carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g., nanotubes (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO)) possess prominent antibacterial properties because of their cytotoxic action of physical piercing and oxidative stress [ [10–13]]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been added to the polyamide layer of a TFC membrane to improve its antibacterial and antifouling properties [14]. However, bio-contaminants deposited on the membrane surface can impact the antibacterial performance of contact-based antibacterial agents, and the use of release-based antibacterial agents (e.g., Ag nanoparticles, Ag NPs) is sometimes more advantageous [15]. It has been reported that the biofouling resistance of polyamide membranes containing Ag NPs was significantly improved [16,17]. However, the release of silver ions (Ag+) from Ag NPs makes it difficult to maintain a long-term antifouling and antibacterial performance, needless to say that an excess amount of Ag+ in water are cytotoxic to living organisms.

Therefore, core-shell nanoparticles with Ag as the core that is coated with another material are expected to help solve the above problems. Using appropriate coatings, core-shell nanoparticles with different physical and chemical properties can be obtained which are suitable for the modification of various membranes [18]. Ag@teapolyphenols (TP) complex nanoparticles have been assembled by using TP, a natural antioxidant, as the shell, and they showed excellent low toxicity for selective antibacterial treatment [19]. In addition, metal@metal oxide core-shell nanoparticles have also shown effective antibacterial properties. For example, Ag@Fe2O3 core-shell NPs exhibited a broad spectrum of bactericidal effects due to slow diffusion of Ag+ out of the Fe2O3 shell [20].

In this study, a new hydrophobic nanoparticle, Ag@ZnO-Oleic acid nanoparticles (Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs) were synthesized. They were allowed to react with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the organic monomer. For preparation of TFN membranes via interfacial polymerization (IP) with piperazine (PIP), thereby incorporating Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs into the polyamide layer. The morphology of the Ag@ZnO NPs and bonding mode with OAc and TMC were investigated in details. The surface chemistry of the membrane and the extent of the reaction between PIP and TMC were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The separation performance and antifouling properties of the membrane were also evaluated to determine its suitability for long-term filtration applications. Moreover, we also investigated long-term release of Ag+ from the cores and the antibacterial properties of the membranes were also investigated by using Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus as model organisms. This work attempts to provide a novel design of TNF membranes with improved antifouling and antibacterial properties for water treatment.

Flat Polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membranes with a nonwoven fabric support rated to have a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 20000 Da was supplied by Synder Membrane Technology Co., Ltd. (Taizhou, China). Silver acetate (Ag(ac)), zinc acetylacetonate (Zn(acac)2), trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and oleic acid (OAc) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-dodecanol was obtained from Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and isopropyl alcohol were supplied by Fengchuan Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Hexane and piperazine (PIP) were purchased from Kemiou Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, MW67000) was supplied by Zhengjiang High-Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). E. coli and S. aureus were used for the anti-bacterial experiments were provided by Tianjin Medical University (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.2) was confected with following salts (NaCl: 8.00 g/L, KCl: 0.20 g/L, Na2HPO4: 1.56 g/L and KH2PO4: 0.20 g/L) which were purchased from Fengchuan Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag-OAc and Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The Ag nanoparticles were synthesized by microemulsion method. First, 1-dodecanol (5 mL) and oleic acid (1 mL) were mixed under stirring in a 50 mL three-neck flask, and the mixture was heated to 210 °C. After addition of silver acetate (50 mg), the reaction was allowed to occur for 1 h. Finally, the dark brown mixture was cooled to room temperature and precipitated by adding ethanol, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm. The reaction products were further purified by washing with hexane and ethanol 2–3 times. The suspension of the Ag nanoparticles was obtained by dispersing the product in hexane.

The core-shell structured Ag@ZnO-OAc nanoparticles were synthesized using a typical two-step procedure. First, 1-dodecanol (5 mL) and oleic acid (1 mL) were mixed under stirring in a 50 mL three-neck flask, and the mixture was heated to 210 °C, following by addition of silver acetate (50 mg). The reaction was allowed to occur for 1 h before cooling to 150 °C, and then zinc acetylacetonate (150 mg) was added to the reaction mixture and reaction continued for an additional 2 h. Finally, the dark brown mixture was cooled to room temperature and precipitated by adding ethanol, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm. The reaction products were further purified by washing with hexane and ethanol 2–3 times. The suspension of the Ag@ZnO nanoparticles was obtained by dispersing the product in hexane.

The bonding mode between Ag@ZnO (or Ag) and OAc was changed by a dehydration condensation reaction via heat treatment. Ag@ZnO (or Ag) nanoparticles (1 g), oleic acid (1.12 mL) and hexane (50 mL) were mixed at 60 °C for 4 h in a 100 mL three-neck flask. The mixture was stirred with isopropyl alcohol (150 mL) and then removed isopropyl alcohol with water by extraction. Finally, Ag@ZnO-OAc (or Ag-OAc) nanoparticles were dried in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h.

The PES ultrafiltration membrane was used as porous support for TFN membranes. After immersing in pure water for 24 h, the PES membrane was soaked in a 2 wt% PIP aqueous solution for 5 min, and excess liquid on the membrane surface was removed with sweeping air. Then, the membrane substrate impregnated with PIP was immersed in a 0.15 wt% TMC/n-hexane solution with different contents of Ag@ZnO-OAc (or Ag-OAc) NPs at room temperature for 1 min to form a thin PA layer on the substrate surface, thereby forming a TFN membrane via interfacial reaction. Then the membrane was subjected heat treatment at 60 °C for 1 min for further crosslinking before being rinsed with deionized water. The membranes so produced were stored in deionized water for further testing. The concentration of Ag-OAc NPs in organic phase was 0.3 wt% to prepare Ag TFN membrane. The concentrations of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs in the organic phase for interfacial polymerization were 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 wt%, and the resulting Ag@ZnO-OAc TFN membranes were designated as N1, N5, N10, N30, and N50 TFN membranes, respectively.

To characterize the size and morphology of the Ag@ZnO NPs, samples were prepared by drying a drop of the suspension of Ag@ZnO-OAc in hexane in a dark room on 230 mesh amorphous carbon-coated copper grids and examined under transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Company, USA) with a lanthanum hexaboride electron gun operating at 120 kV. Chemical characterizations were carried out using Lambda-25 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the bonding mode between Ag@ZnO NPs and OAc and between Ag@ZnO-OAc and TMC. The XPS measurements were performed on a Quanta 200 spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV photons) at a pass energy of 93.9 eV. Survey spectra were run in the binding energy range of 0–800 eV, followed by high resolution scans of the C 1s and O 1s regions.

The chemical composition of the membrane surface and the degree of reaction between PIP and TMC were determined by using XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The surface morphologies and thicknesses of the membranes were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Gemini SEM500, Germany) operating at 10 kV. The specimens were sputter-coated with gold.

The surface topography and roughness of the membranes were also observed under an atomic force microscopy (AFM, Agilent 5500, USA). The surface roughness parameters were evaluated in terms of the root mean square (RMS) of roughness based on a 5.0 μm × 5.0 μm scan area.

SurPASS electrodynamic analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria) was used to measure the electronegativity of the membranes surface using a 0.001 M KCl feed solution at 25 °C. Before testing, the membrane samples were equilibrated in a 0.001 M KCl solution for at least 12 h. The pH value of the wet membrane surface was adjusted using a hydrochloric acid (HCl) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution during the measurement.

The surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was evaluated by contact angle measurements (Drop Shape Analysis 100, KRUSS GmbH Co., Germany), which were conducted using a sessile drop method at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity. The reported contact angle values and standard deviations were based on at least 5 measurements of each membrane sample.

The separation performance of the membrane was evaluated by using cross-flow filtration. The water flux and the solute rejection of the membranes were measured with pure water and aqueous solutions of Na2SO4 and NaCl at a concentration 1000 mg/L after pre-filtration at 25 °C and 5 bar for 0.5 h. The electrical conductivities of feed and permeate solutions were measured by using a conductivity meter. Water flux (Jw, LMH) and salt interception (R) are calculated from the following equations:
(1)
J
w
=
V
A × Δ t
(2)
R =
(
1 −
C
p
C
f
)
× 100 %
where V is the volume of the permeate collected (L), A is the effective area of the membrane (m2), and t is the permeation time. Cp and Cf are the solute concentrations in permeate and feed solutions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of membrane antifouling and anti-bacterial properties

ce:section.ce:para: The antifouling performance of the membrane was investigated at 25 °C and 5 bar using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a model foulant. For this filtration test, pure water was used to get a steady initial pure water flux for 0.5 h. Then the membranes were subjected to a BSA/PBS solution (1 g/L, pH = 7.4) for 0.5 h, followed by rinsing with pure water for 1 min before cross flow filtration with pure water permeation for 0.5 h. This process was repeated for 3 times to complete a test cycle, and permeation flux was measured every 5 min. The initial flux of pure water was recorded as JW0, other fluxes of pure water were recorded as JW1, JW2 and JW3, and fluxes of BSA/PBS solution were recorded as JB1, JB2 and JB3 where 1,2,3 is the number of cycles.

Normally, higher flux recovery ratio (FRR) shows better antifouling property of the nanofiltration membranes. Moreover, in order to analyze the fouling process in details, total flux decline ratio (DRt), reversible flux decline ratio (DRr) and irreversible flux decline ratio (DRir) were calculated. Evaluations of FRR, DRt, DRr and DRir are as follows:
(3)
F R R =
J
W i
J
W i − 1
× 100 %
(4)
D
R
t
=
J
W i − 1
−
J
B i
J
W i − 1
× 100 %
(5)
D
R
r
=
J
W i
−
J
B i
J
W i − 1
× 100 %
(6)
D
R
i r
=
J
W i − 1
−
J
W i
J
W i − 1
× 100 %
where i is the number of cycles.

The anti-bacterial properties of the membranes were evaluated by measuring the effects of the membranes to the activities of Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus. Bacterial solutions at a concentration of 4 × 107 CFU/mL were diluted to 4 × 105 CFU/mL with the PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Membrane samples with an area of 4 cm2 were sterilized with ultraviolet light before being immersed into a 20 mL of the bacterial solution, followed by cultivation at 37 °C for 24 h. The concentration of the bacterial solution was then measured using a microplate spectrophotometer at 600 nm, and the bacterial activity of the membrane was determined by
(7)
B a c t e r i a l
v i a b i l i t y =
A
A
0
× 100 %
where A0 and A are the absorbances of the bacterial solution before and after contacting the membrane, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Releasing of silver ions (Ag+) from the Ag@ZnO-OAc TFN membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the releasing rate of Ag+ from the Ag@ZnO-OAc TFN membrane, A N30 TFN membrane sample with a surface area of 4 cm2 was immersed in a light-shielded bottle with 20 mL of deionized water placed in a thermostatic shaker at 50 rpm and 25 °C, and the solution was replaced by new deionized water every 24 h. The solution collected was acidified with a 5% HNO3 solution, and the Ag content in the solution was determined by using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). In another set of experiment, a N30 TFN film sample of 4 cm2 was immersed in a 5% HNO3 solution placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to dissolve all the Ag on the membrane completely, and the total amount of Ag loaded on the membrane was also measured by the ICP-MS.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and HRTEM image of Ag NPs and Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs. The UV absorption peak of Ag NPs occurred at 405 nm, while UV absorption peak of Ag in the Ag@ZnO NPs occurred at 411 nm, and the shift indicates the larger particle sizes of Ag@ZnO NPs [21]. No characteristic peak of ZnO was observed for Ag@ZnO NPs at 370 nm, indicating amorphous structure of ZnO. Fig. 1B shows that both Ag NPs and Ag@ZnO NPs were well dispersed in n-hexane. The Ag NPs were spherical crystals with an average diameter of 7.8 ± 1.8 nm, while the core-shell Ag@ZnO NPs were 8.42 ± 2.72 nm in diameter with a nano-silver core and a thin ZnO shell. The thickness of the amorphous ZnO shell surrounding the Ag core was estimated to be around 1 nm, which was consistent with the results of UV–vis absorption spectra.

The hydrophobic Ag@ZnO NPs were coated with a layer of OAc, and the carboxyl groups of OAc would react with acyl chloride groups of TMC, leading to reduced dispersibility of Ag@ZnO NPs in hexane. Therefore, attempt was made to consume the carboxyl groups of OAc by changing the bonding mode between Ag@ZnO and OAc through heat treatment. Fig. 2 A shows the FTIR spectra of Ag@ZnO nanoparticles in hexane before and after heat treatment and Ag@ZnO nanoparticles in TMC-hexane solution after heat treatment. As shown in Fig. 2A (a), before heat treatment, the peak at 3440 cm−1 was stretching vibration of –OH from the surface of ZnO and OAc. The peaks at 1400, 1460, 2925 and 2850 cm−1 were C–H bending vibration, C–H antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration, respectively. The peak at 1538 cm−1 could be due to the CC stretching. The peak at 1625 cm−1 was stretching vibration of CO, which was related to the use of zinc acetylacetonate. It was obvious that the peak of –OH at 3440 cm−1 became weaker and a new peak appeared at 1714 cm−1 for CO stretching from carboxylic acid after heat treatment (Fig. 2A (b)), suggesting that the reaction between Ag@ZnO NPs and OAc occurred between –OH of Ag@ZnO and –COOH of OAc to form bidentate carboxylates or monodentate carboxylates [22], as illustrated in Fig. 2B. When the heat-treated nanoparticles were added into the TMC-hexane solution, the peak at 1538 cm−1 disappeared and a new peak at 1625 cm−1 for CO stretching vibration was observed (Fig. 2A(c)), which indicated that the CC of OAc on Ag@ZnO-OAc particles reacted with the acyl chloride of TMC to form CO [23]. And the peak at 3010 cm−1 corresponding to = C–H of OAc was shown in Fig. 1A(b), but not visible in Fig. 2A(c). It also indicated the reaction between CC of OAc with acyl chloride group of TMC that is schematically depicted in Fig. 5. At the same time, a broad peak at 3400 cm−1 appeared again because the acyl chloride of TMC was unstable and easily reacted with water in the air to form carboxyl groups. These results confirm that the bonding between Ag@ZnO and OAc was successfully changed from hydrogen bond to chemical bond by heat treatment, which helped to improve the dispersion of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs in the TMC-hexane solution desired for the interfacial polymerization for membrane formation.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS C 1s and O 1s spectra of the raw NPs (before heat treatment) and modified NPs (after heat treatment). The binding energy and area percentage of the deconvoluted XPS C 1s and O 1s peaks are summarized in Table 1 . It was shown that the connection between Ag@ZnO NPs and OAc was mainly bidentate carboxylates, which is consistent with the results reported by Wilson et al. [24] and Rakhshan et al. [23].

The XPS full spectra of TFC and Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membranes are shown in Fig. 4
. Compared with pristine TFC membranes, the peaks of Zn and Ag elements appeared in the TFN membranes as a result of the Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs used. As expected, with an increase in the content of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs, the atomic percentages of Zn and Ag in the TFN membranes increased, whereas the atomic percentages of N decreased.

The interfacial polymerization process of TFN membranes is illustrated in Fig. 5. Generally, the Oxygen/nitrogen ratio (O/N) is usually used to measure the cross-linking degree of interfacial polymerization [25,26]. However, direct calculation of the O/N ratio was very inaccurate because of the large amount of oxygen in bidentate carboxylate formed as a result of the reaction between ZnO and OAc. Thus, oxygen in bidentate carboxylate was excluded in the calculation of O/N ratio by means of XPS-peak-differentiation-imitating analysis of O, as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2 . Because the peak positions of CO from bidentate carboxylate and OC–N overlapped and each OC–N contained one N atom, the percentage of O atoms excluding oxygen from bidentate carboxylate was given by:
(8)
A t . %
(
O '
)
= A t . %
(
O
)
− A t . %
(
O
)
× A r e a %
( C = O , O = C − N )
+ A t . %
( N )
where At.%(O) and At.%(N) are the percentages of oxygen and nitrogen atoms on the surface of the membrane, respectively, and Area% (CO, OC–N) is the (CO, OC–N) peak area percentage of XPS O 1s spectrum. Therefore, the O/N ratio of the TFN membranes was calculated using the following equation:
(9)
O / N =
A t . %
( O ' )
A t . %
( N )

The calculated results are presented in Table 2. When the concentration of nanoparticles was less than 0.05 wt%, the O/N ratio decreased with an increase in the nanoparticle concentration, and the degree of cross-linking on the membrane surface became higher. However, when the concentration of nanoparticles was more than 0.05 wt%, the O/N ratio increased with an increase in the nanoparticle concentration, and the degree of cross-linking on the membrane surface became lower. During interfacial polymerization, PIP in the aqueous phase diffused to the organic phase through the pristine PA layer formed at the beginning [27]. Continued reaction between PIP and TMC resulted in a dense PA on the surface of PES membrane [28]. The addition of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs impacted the interfacial TMC/PIP reaction, and the surface properties of the TFN membranes were affected. On the one hand, portions of acyl chloride groups in TMC were used by reacting with CC of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs to form carbonyl groups before interfacial polymerization, which tended to result in a loosely packed PA layer being formed in the initial stage of the interfacial polymerization. Therefore, PIP was able to diffuse through the pristine PA layer readily to react with TMC in the organic phase. On the other hand, the hydrophobic Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs rendered PA layer formed at the initial stage of interfacial polymerization less hydrophilic, which made PIP diffusion through the pristine PA layer more difficult to reach TMC in the organic phase. Because of these two opposite effects, the formation of loosely packed PA layer was predominant when a low content of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs was used. The addition of the nanoparticles accelerated the diffusion of PIP, and the cross-linking degree of the membrane surface increased. If the nanoparticle content was high enough, the increased hydrophobicity would be predominant, resulting in slow PIP diffusion and reduced cross-linking degree of the membrane surface.

To further explore the influence of incorporating Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs into polyamide thin film membrane, the surface morphology of the membrane was examined under FESEM. The surface images of TFC and TFN membranes are shown in Fig. 7 , where granular bumps were observed, which were characteristic of polyamide thin film composite membranes formed by interfacial polymerization of TMC and PIP. At a concentration of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs equal or less than 0.1 wt%, the granular bulges on the membrane surface became smaller with an increase in the nanoparticle content. Cross-linking degree of membrane surface decreased, and the membrane surface tended to be smoother [29]. However, when the negatively charged nanoparticle concentration was sufficiently high, the negative electricity of initial PA layer increased. A large number of nodular structures formed on the membrane surface, and the surface roughness increased significantly.

Fig. 8 shows the cross-sectional morphologies of the TFC and TFN membranes. The functional layers of TFN membranes are largely uniform and continuous. However, too high a concentration of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs could cause particle agglomeration, and because Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs were connected to TMC, the resulting polyamide layer formed by interfacial polymerization would have an uneven yet continuous surface, as observed for membrane N50 TFN. Table 3 shows the thicknesses of the polyamide layers in the TFC and TFN membranes estimated from the FESEM images. It is shown that the polyamide layer was thinner with an increase in the content of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs used in interfacial polymerization at nanoparticle concentrations less than 0.3 wt%. However, at nanoparticle concentrations more than 0.3 wt%, there was significant change in the polyamide functional layer. The polyamide functional layer became loose and the thickness of functional layer was greatly increased.

Fig. 9 shows the AFM images of TFC and TFN membranes, and the surface roughness of the membranes based on the AFM images was shown in Table 3. The hydrophilicity of the membrane surface was evaluated by measuring the Young's contact angle (Θ), and the results are also shown in Table 3. The effects of surface roughness on the water contact angle may be corrected by the Wenzel equation to calculate the apparent contact angle (Θ*). Because of the incorporation of hydrophobic Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs which had a surface filled with long alkane chains, the TFN membranes had a more hydrophobic surface than the TFC membrane. With an increase in the concentration of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs, the apparent water contact angle on the surface of the TFN membranes increased and the surface hydrophilicity of the membrane decreased.

Fig. 10 shows the zeta potential of the surfaces of the TFC and TFN membranes. The polyamide surface layer contains a large number of amino and carboxyl terminal groups. At a pH below the isoelectric point, the amino terminal groups are protonated to make the membrane surface charged positively; above the isoelectric point, the carboxyl terminal groups are dissociated to make the membrane surface charged negatively [30,31]. As shown in Fig. 10, the TFC membrane had an isoelectric point of 3.2, while the isoelectric points of nanoparticle incorporated TFN membranes were between 3.5 and 3.7, similar to the results reported in the literature [32,33]. As shown in Table 2, the percentage of nitrogen atoms decreased with an increase in the nanoparticle concentration. In addition, a higher degree of cross-linking means a fewer number of terminal groups formed. These two factors led to a decrease in the content of amino terminal groups, the difficulty of protonation, and the shift of isoelectric point to a higher pH. The Zeta potential of the membrane surface decreased with an increase in pH. Due to the deprotonation of amino terminal groups under acidic conditions, the positive charges gathered on the membrane surface and the zeta potential increased with decreasing pH. Under alkaline conditions, the carboxyl terminal groups were dissociated into COO−, and the zeta potential decreased in the direction of high pH with negative charges gathered on the membrane surface.

The effects of the concentration of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs on the permeability of TFN membranes are shown in Fig. 11 A. The pristine TFC membrane had a pure water flux of 35.9 LMH, and the membrane rejections to Na2SO4 and NaCl were 94.54% and 39.47%, respectively. With an increase in the NP concentration, pure water flux of the TFN membrane decreased to 27.4 LMH and then increased. The opposite trend in salt rejection was observed. The highest salt rejection of the N30 TFN membrane was 98.84% for Na2SO4 and 59.49% for NaCl. The influences of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs on the separation performance of the TFN membranes were mainly due to the following reasons. On the one hand, Ag@ZnO-OAc NP surfaces were filled with long alkane chains, which reduces the hydrophilicity of the membranes. On the other hand, the increase in the membrane surface roughness enlarged the effective contact area between the membrane and water molecules, which favors water permeation. The latter aspect appears to be predominant for the changes in water flux. In addition, the presence of proper amount of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs enhances the electronegativity of the membrane surface, that is helpful to get a higher salt rejection. However, if too much NPs are there, the PA layer can be uneven, discontinuous and even defective, resulting in a higher water flux but low salt rejection. This seems to be the case for N50 TFN membrane.

For practical applications, fouling resistance of the membrane is also an important factor [34]. The anti-fouling performance of the TFC and TFN membranes was evaluated by using BSA as a model foulant [35]. Fig. 11B shows the normal flux over time for BSA filtration. When the membranes are fouled, water flux decreases rapidly due to the adsorption and deposition of the foulants on the membrane surface. The normalized flux of TFC membranes decreased more significantly than that of the TFN membranes with an appropriate amount of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs. After cleaning with deionized water for 1 min, the normalized flux of the TFN membrane embedded with Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs showed a higher flux recovery. Water fluxes of the N5, N10 and N30 TFN membranes remained above 90% of original water flux after three cycles, which may be attributed to the negative charge of the NPs that help to repel negatively charged BSA. As shown earlier, both N10 and N30 TFN membranes showed strong negative charges (pH = 7). Furthermore, smother surfaces for N5 and N10 TFN membranes make it more difficult for the foulant to depose on membrane surfaces. The N50 TFN membrane, which has the maximum roughness and lowest electronegativity (pH = 7), showed the lowest flux recovery.

The fouling resistance of the membrane as expressed in terms of FRR, DRt, DRr and DRir are shown in Fig. 12 . Higher FRR and lower DRt and DRir values indicate better fouling resistance of the membrane. In the first cycle, the FRR, DRt, DRr and DRir values of the TFC membrane were 88.5%, 21.6%, 10.1% and 11.5%, respectively. With incorporation of proper amounts of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs in the membrane, the FRR value increased while the DRr, DRt and DRir values decreased. That is, the anti-fouling performance of the TFN membrane improved. For instance, the FRR, DRt, DRr and DRir values of the N30 TFN membrane, which showed the best antifouling performance, were 95.77%, 9.96%, 5.73% and 4.23%, respectively. Irreversible membrane fouling was caused by irreversible deposition/adsorption of the foulants on membrane surface and blockage of membrane pores [36]. The incorporation of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs into the membrane reduced the surface roughness of the membrane, improved the negative electricity of the membrane surface, and reduced the interactions between the membrane surface and pollutants. Thus, the membrane became more resistant to fouling. Note that the poor anti-fouling performance of the N50 TFN membrane was due to excessive roughness and low negative electricity of the membrane surface. After three cycles, the N30 TFN membrane still maintained a high flux and 94.4% flux recovery. However, the FRR value of the TFC membrane gradually decreased to a lower level (80.9%). The cyclic fouling tests confirmed the good reusability and durability of the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membranes.

The antimicrobial properties of the membranes are shown in Fig. 13 A, where the bacteriostatic efficacy of the TFN membranes was shown to increase significantly with an increase in the amount of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs contained in the membranes. At NP concentration of above 0.3%, the antibacterial rates of the TFN membranes to E. coli and S. aureus were above 99.9%. The excellent bactericidal ability of the membrane was due to the synergistic bactericidal effects of ZnO and Ag. One part of the effects was caused by a variety of active substances (e.g., hydroxyl radicals and reactive oxygen) produced from ZnO irradiated via ultraviolet light [37]. Hydroxyl radicals and reactive oxygen had reduction, for example, hydroxyl ions, oxygen anions and hydrogen peroxide. These oxidants inhibited bacterial viability by destroying the outer membrane of the bacteria. The other part was induced by Ag+ released from NPs. Ag+ activated oxygen from air or water to produce hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals, which could further oxidize and kill bacteria [38]. Therefore, the introduction of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs made TFN membranes have better antifouling and antibacterial performance. Since the antibacterial rates of the Ag TFN membranes to E. coli and S. aureus were above 99.9%, it is necessary to investigate the stability of NPs into the TFN membranes.

In order to evaluate the stability of the Ag@ZnO-OAc and Ag-OAc NPs in the PA layer of the TFN membranes, the release behavior of Ag+ was analyzed under simulated water environment for several days. Fig. 13B shows the release behavior of Ag+ from the N30 TFN and Ag TFN membranes. Overall the release rate of Ag+ from Ag TFN membrane was faster than N30 TFN membrane while the release rate of Ag+ from the PA layer of N30 TFN membrane was very slow. After 9 days, the Ag+ release rate of N30 TFN membrane was largely constant at 0.016 ± 0.001 μg cm−2 day−1, and the antibacterial rates to E. coli and S. aureus were still above 99.9%. After 23 days, the Ag+ release rate of Ag TFN membrane was below 0.01 μg cm−2 day−1. However, the antibacterial rate to E. coli was 93.8%, the antibacterial rate to S. aureus was 41.2%. Moreover, the total release of Ag+ was far below the national standard of drinking water (0.05 mg/L, which is harmless to human body). Furthermore, the N30 TFN membrane showed that the Ag content was 5.38 × 10−3 ± 0.17 mg cm−2 and antimicrobial activity lasted for more than 300 days. According to the Ag content and the surface elemental percentage for Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs shown in Table 4 , the content of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs incorporated in the N30 membranes was given by:
(10)
m
N P s
=
m
A g
A t . %
( A g )
where mAg is the Ag content of the N30 TFN membranes, and At.%(Ag) is the percentages of Ag atoms on the Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs.

After calculation, the content of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs incorporated in the N30 membranes was 0.87 ± 0.03 mg cm−2. The results indicated that Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs on the TFN membranes maintained a slow Ag+ release rate for a prolonged period of time, thereby achieving long-term bactericidal performance.

Table 5 compares the antifouling and antibacterial performance of TFN membranes with different nanofillers. The TFN membrane in this work (N30 TFN membrane) exhibited excellent antifouling and antibacterial activities, which play an important role of the TFN membranes for water treatment.

In this paper, Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs were synthesized, characterized and incorporated into the polyamide layer of TFN membranes during the course of interfacial polymerization. Incorporating Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs into the PA layer was shown to improve the chemical and physical properties of the resulting TFN membranes. The salt rejections of the membrane to Na2SO4 and NaCl as high as 98.84% and 59.49% were obtained respectively, which were considerably higher than those of the pristine TFC membrane. Compared with the pristine TFC membrane, the TFN membrane embedded with the nanoparticles showed a lower flux decline rate when tested with BSA solution filtration and higher flux recovery rate after water flush. When the concentration of Ag@ZnO-OAc NPs was 0.3% in membrane fabrication, over 99.9% of E. coli and S. aureus were killed. The membrane also showed a release rate of Ag+ over a long period of time. With good stability, improved fouling resistance and antibacterial performance, the Ag@ZnO-OAc/PA TFN membranes showed great potential for water treatment applications.

Xiujing Huang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yingbo Chen: Conceptualization, Software, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Xianshe Feng: Visualization, Investigation. Xiaoyu Hu: Supervision. Yufeng Zhang: Project administration. Lei Liu: Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Alam, Graphene oxide-silver nanosheet-incorporated polyamide thin-film composite membranes for antifouling and antibacterial action against Escherichia coli and bovine serum albumin, J. Ind. Eng. Chem. 80 (2019) 227-238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Palygorskite/silver nanoparticles incorporated polyamide thin film nanocomposite membranes with enhanced water permeating, antifouling and antimicrobial performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, Y. Li, W. Wang, B. Gao, Z. Wang, Palygorskite/silver nanoparticles incorporated polyamide thin film nanocomposite membranes with enhanced water permeating, antifouling and antimicrobial performance, Chemosphere 236 (2019) 124396.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biofouling mitigation effect of thin film nanocomposite membranes immobilized with laponite mediated metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Li, L. Yu, Z. Xiao, C. Jiang, B. Gao, Z. Wang, Biofouling mitigation effect of thin film nanocomposite membranes immobilized with laponite mediated metal ions, Desalination 473 (2020) 114162.
